mappings.rationaleAI: The Mexican Revolution from 1910 through regime consolidation in the 1920s and radical reforms of the 1930s directly addresses Obregon's coup and reforms (A.1) and Cardenas' social and economic reforms including expropriation of foreign petroleum (A.2).

Brazil's political, economic, and social transformation from mid-twentieth century, democratic development, and international positioning directly address political, social, and economic developments in Brazil since 1945 (G.2).
Latin American writers—Borges, Garcia Marquez, Neruda—gave voice to the region's political and social upheavals, providing cultural context for the period since 1920.
Revolutionary theory and communist revolutions connect to the Mexican Revolution and its aftermath (A.1) and the Cuban Revolution of 1959 and Castro's program for Cuban Socialism (B.2.c.ii).
Intrastate armed conflicts, guerrilla warfare, and peacebuilding connect to El Salvador's civil war (B.1.c) and La Violencia in Colombia and left-wing guerrilla movements (C.2).

Authoritarian regimes including military and personalist types, with Argentina as an example, address Haiti's Duvalier dictatorship (B.2.a), Batista's Cuba (B.2.c.i), Pinochet's Chile (E.3.b), the Peron era in Argentina (F.1.b), and Vargas' Brazil (G.1).

Cuban communism, Ho Chi Minh, and Cold War confrontation with the United States address the Cuban Revolution and Castro's program for Cuban Socialism (B.2.c.ii) and Cuba under Castro including alignment with the Soviet bloc (B.2.c.iii).
Cuban communism as a case study and socialism's relationship to revolutionary politics address Cuba under Castro (B.2.c.ii) and Allende's Marxist administration in Chile (E.3.b).
Latin American left-wing populist presidents including Peron and Chavez directly address Venezuela under Chavez (C.1) and the Peron era in Argentina (F.1.b).
Democracy as political system and transitions from authoritarianism connect to post-Pinochet moves toward democracy in Chile (E.3.b) and return to civilian rule in Brazil (G.2.e).
Cold War superpower rivalry and proxy wars connect to Cuba's alignment with the Soviet bloc (B.2.c.iii) and U.S.-Cuba confrontations including the Bay of Pigs and Cuban missile crisis (B.2.c.iv).

U.S. foreign policy including imperialism and Cold War engagement addresses Mexico-U.S. relations including NAFTA (A.4.b), U.S. military intervention in Nicaragua (B.1.d), and U.S.-Cuba relations (B.2.c.iv).
Human rights frameworks and anti-discrimination protections connect to Guatemala's scorched-earth counterinsurgency campaigns (B.1.a.iii) and political repression under Pinochet in Chile (E.3.b).
Organized crime including drug trafficking connects to the growth of drug trafficking and associated corruption in Colombia (C.2.c) and drug-related terrorism.

mappings.vsiTitle: The Great Depression and The New Deal
mappings.rationaleAI: The Great Depression's international impact connects to the effect of the Depression on Bolivia's mining industry (D.3.a) and Uruguay's economic decline (F.2.a).
Nationalism and self-determination connect to Mexico's post-revolutionary nationalism (A.1) and independence movements in Central American republics (B.1).
International migration and immigration policy connect to tensions over illegal immigration to the U.S. from Mexico (A.4.b) and Cuban emigration to the U.S. (B.2.c.iv).
Forced migration and refugee crises connect to the influx of Central American refugees into Mexico (A.4.b) and waves of Cuban emigration to the U.S. (B.2.c.iv).
Diplomatic history and international relations connect to U.S.-Cuba diplomatic confrontations including the Bay of Pigs and missile crisis (B.2.c.iv).
Globalization including the Mexico-USA border and NAFTA connects to Mexico's relations with the U.S. and the North American Free Trade Agreement (A.4.b).
Transition from colonial empires to independent nation-states connects to the new nations of the Caribbean region gaining independence (B.2.d).
Global poverty, economic inequality, and development challenges connect to economic crises and development in Ecuador, Peru, and Bolivia (D) and Brazil's social and economic unrest (G.2).
Comparative analysis of global prosperity and poverty connects to Venezuela's oil-based economic boom and decline (C.1) and Brazil's economic development patterns (G).

mappings.vsiAuthor: John O. S. Wilson, John Goddard
mappings.rationaleAI: Banking systems, financial crises, and sovereign debt connect to Mexico's currency devaluation, severe recession, and debt crisis (A.4.a).
Neoliberal economic paradigm including deregulation and privatization connects to Mexico's economic reforms in the 1980s and 1990s (A.4.a) and Chile's post-Pinochet economic development (E.3.b).
Capitalism and resource-based economies connect to Venezuela's nationalization of the oil industry and economic fluctuations (C.1.b).
Empire as political formation and neo-imperialism connect to U.S. and European territories and possessions in the Caribbean region (B.2.e).
Covers Brazil's role in the BRICS grouping challenging Western-dominated institutions, relevant to Brazil's contemporary international positioning after return to civilian rule.
Covers far-right movements in the Americas and corporatism, relevant to Argentina's conservative restoration (1930-43) and the Peron era with its fascist-influenced political dynamics.
